items.correct: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to INCREASE a behaviour.
items.misconception: Punishing a behaviour to decrease it.
items.random_wrong: Giving a reward to decrease behaviour.
items.why: Students confuse negative reinforcement with punishment. Both involve something unpleasant, but reinforcement (positive or negative) always INCREASES behaviour; punishment decreases it.

items.q: What did Bowlby mean by monotropy?
items.correct: One primary attachment figure (usually the mother) is qualitatively different and most important.
items.misconception: A child can only ever form one attachment.
items.why: Students take 'mono' literally as 'only one'. Bowlby recognised multiple attachments but argued the primary one was uniquely important for the internal working model.

items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.

items.q: Bandura's study used what kind of doll?
items.why: It's Bobo Doll. “Action figure” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Seeing someone else rewarded for an act is:
items.why: Correct answer: Vicarious reinforcement. “Direct reinforcement” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many stages does SLT name in the learning process?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
